title: RuBisCO
alt: Bruce Schneier believes safewords are fundamentally insecure and recommends that you ask your partner to stop via public key signature.
transcript:
[Person in background (out-of-frame) screams out this word over all 3 panels.]

[Cueball is working on a laptop at a desk. Megan is reading a book in an armchair.]
Person 1: RIBULOSEBISPH..

[In a frameless panel, Cueball has stopped working. Megan has stopped reading, and is holding her book.]
Person 1: ...OSPHATECARBOXYL...

[Cueball continues working. Megan resumes reading her book.]
Person 1: ...ASEOXYGENASE!
Person 2: Oh, Sorry!
Megan: Man, chemists pick the worst safewords.
explanation:
are designated words for sexual play which are meant to be called if one partner is uncomfortable with the way things are proceeding as alternatives to simply saying "no" or "stop", which may be used to express playacted reluctance by a submissive partner who actually wants to continue. Calling the pre-chosen "safe word" would be a sign to stop. To prevent accidental usage, people generally pick words that they wouldn't normally use, such as "Pineapple" or "Hedgehog." In the case of this comic, the characters are chemists, and the uncommon word they happen to have chosen is , also known as RuBisCO (which actually isn't a very uncommon word in the scientific world, as it's the most abundant  on earth, but it would be uncommon to use the full word). However, the length of the word makes it impractical for a safe word, as it would take too long to say; indeed, using the shorter form "RuBisCO" would normally be a fine safe word.

The joke of this comic is that the first thing the reader sees is a seemingly random long word spoken, with the speaker off screen. The meaning is mysterious until Megan reveals it. This appears to be a regular occurrence, as Cueball and Megan go back to work with no questions asked.

The title text mentions , a computer security professional, and public keys which is the publicly known half of , which uses two mathematically linked keys to decrypt information. The joke is that Schneier considers safewords as a type of security and thus believes they are not safe enough and recommends the key signature. However, whereas it takes a finite (albeit long) time to say RuBisCO in full during your submission, it would be impossible to use any public keys to stop your partner.

Randall later in 1128: Fifty Shades referenced the book  which made the concept of  perhaps more widely known to the mainstream public. Here the word is very short: Red.
